planting.note: Direct sow in fall after cold stratification or scarification in dry, lean, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Slender Lespedeza is a slender, upright native perennial of dry, open woods, barrens, and forest edges throughout the eastern and central US. It produces narrow trifoliate leaves and dense axillary clusters of small rose-purple pea flowers from July through September, followed by single-seeded pods that are eaten heavily by quail and small birds. Sow seeds in fall in lean, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun, scarifying or cold-stratifying first to break dormancy. Once established, it is highly drought-tolerant and serves as a valuable late-season native legume for wildlife food plots and dry native meadow gardens.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Lespedeza virginica

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; scarify or cold-stratify seeds to improve germination.

directions: Hairy Lespedeza is a robust, erect native perennial with stiffly hairy stems, large trifoliate leaves, and dense rounded clusters of white to cream-white pea flowers from August through October, making it one of the latest-blooming native legumes in the eastern US. It is native to dry, open woods, pine barrens, and acidic sandy or rocky soil throughout eastern North America, and its late flowers provide critical nectar for fall-flying native bees while the seeds are prime food for quail, turkey, and sparrows. Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, slightly acidic, well-drained soil, scarifying seeds beforehand to encourage germination. Plants are highly drought-tolerant once established and require minimal care in sandy or rocky garden soils.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Lespedeza hirta

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, dry, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; cold stratification improves germination rates.

directions: Creeping Lespedeza is a prostrate to spreading native perennial forming low, wiry mats of stems with small, rounded trifoliate leaflets and small pink-purple pea flowers from July through September. It is native to dry, open, sandy or rocky, acidic soil in open woods, barrens, and power-line rights-of-way throughout the eastern US, and its low-growing habit makes it distinctly useful as a drought-tolerant native groundcover on infertile slopes and sandy garden beds. Direct sow in fall in lean, dry, acidic, well-drained soil; cold stratification prior to sowing is beneficial. Plants spread slowly from rhizomes and are highly drought-tolerant once established, requiring little to no supplemental irrigation or fertilization.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Lespedeza repens

planting.note: Transplant nursery-propagated stock in spring into dry, sandy, acidic, nutrient-poor, well-drained soil in full sun; scarify seeds before starting indoors.

directions: Hairy Wild Indigo is a federally endangered native wild indigo endemic to a small number of longleaf pine flatwoods sites in Brantley County, Georgia, with a total wild population numbering only in the hundreds of individuals. It produces densely hairy, gray-green foliage covered in distinctive spider-web-like (arachnoid) hairs and yellow pea flowers in spring, requiring dry, sandy, acidic, nutrient-poor, very well-drained soil in full sun — the same extreme conditions found in intact longleaf pine flatwoods. Obtain only nursery-propagated plants from reputable specialty native plant nurseries and transplant into the garden in spring, as seed-grown plants typically require two to three years to reach first bloom. Cultivating nursery stock of this species is a meaningful act of conservation for one of the rarest plants in the United States.
sources: Center for Plant Conservation - Baptisia arachnifera

planting.note: Direct sow in fall after scarifying and cold-stratifying seeds; plant in well-drained soil in full sun and allow natural winter conditions to complete stratification.

directions: Mountain Golden Banner is a showy, robust native perennial in the pea family, producing stout leafy stems with trifoliate lupine-like leaves and dense upright racemes of bright golden-yellow pea flowers in May and June — one of the most striking yellow wildflowers of the Rocky Mountain landscape. It is native to mountain meadows, open slopes, and forest clearings from Montana to New Mexico, spreading by rhizomes to form large, persistent colonies over time. Direct sow in fall after scarifying and cold-stratifying seeds, in well-drained soil in full sun; seeds may take one to two seasons to germinate and plants typically reach first bloom in their second year. This species is intolerant of summer heat and humidity and is not suited to gardens south or east of USDA zone 7, making it ideal for Rocky Mountain and intermountain western gardens.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Thermopsis montana
